questions.text: I often speak with an air of finality that signals the discussion is over.
I experience anger as a physical heat that radiates through my body.
I believe that the discipline of maintaining emotional equilibrium during others' pain is a capacity that serves the common good and deserves deliberate development.
I believe that the most sustainable approach to helping others involves maintaining clear boundaries between their experience and my reasoning.
I am energized by being in the circle and contributing as one voice among many.
I feel that the moral demand of suffering is the reality that has most decisively influenced the causes I support and the relationships I cultivate.
I find that my natural resting state is one of calm alertness, not agitation or anticipation.
I think that approaching difficult human situations with a clear and logical framework is the approach that produces the most sustainable solutions.
I prefer joint ownership of outcomes so that no single person bears the weight.
I prefer to let norms emerge organically as people work together.
I can usually tell when someone is flirting versus just being friendly.
My body stays in a state of relaxed readiness regardless of the emotional temperature around me.
I tend to select projects based on how they will develop my personal expertise.
When entering a new group, I observe how things work before settling into a role.
I feel that a well-directed team outperforms a democratic one.
I find that I am usually unaware of who feels most and least comfortable in a group until it becomes obvious.
I do not naturally sense when a social ritual, like a toast or a round of introductions, carries deeper meaning than its surface function.
I believe that an organized environment reflects and supports an organized mind.
My emotional reactions are vivid enough to produce physical symptoms — headaches, stomach knots, trembling.
I hold the view that self-advocacy is a skill everyone should develop, and I practice it constantly.
I rarely sense when the emotional tone of a text conversation shifts.
I feel that my daily to-do list should have more items for others than for myself.
I tend to take unexpected news in stride without a pronounced emotional spike.
I hold the view that my moral engagement with suffering should be measured by consistency of action, not by intensity of feeling.
I find that remembering small details about other people's lives and acting on them is one of my strengths.
I routinely prioritize my own deadlines over requests that come from others.
I believe that registering others' suffering as important is a fundamental part of my moral compass.
I believe that good habits, built over time, are the backbone of a well-lived life.
I develop professionally by following whatever interests me at any given time.
I notice when someone's politeness has an edge to it.
I notice when a person's speaking pace slows because they are choosing their words around a sensitive listener.
I find that committing too firmly to plans can make me miss out on better alternatives.
I experience anticipatory anxiety as a full-body state, not just a mental worry.
I believe my calendar should reflect other people's needs as much as my own.
I find that I can mobilize people toward a goal effectively.
I design my workflows to minimize friction and maximize repeatability.
